name: dynesty (Dynamic Nested Sampling)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); Josh Speagle / University of Toronto

performance.notes: NumPy-based; multiprocessing support; nested sampling is slower than MCMC for simple problems

agent_readiness.notes: Dynamic nested sampling for Bayesian evidence and posterior estimation. import dynesty. def loglikelihood(theta): return ll. def prior_transform(u): return transform from [0,1]^n to prior. sampler = dynesty.DynamicNestedSampler(loglikelihood, prior_transform, ndim=5). sampler.run_nested(). results = sampler.results. logZ = results.logz[-1]. samples = results.samples. weights = results.importance_weights(). dynesty.plotting.runplot(results). dynesty.plotting.cornerplot(results). Supports multiprocessing: pool=Pool(4), queue_size=4. Static sampler: dynesty.NestedSampler.

gotchas: Slower than MCMC for unimodal posteriors — use emcee when evidence not needed
prior_transform maps unit hypercube to prior — confusing convention
Dynamic version (DynamicNestedSampler) preferred over static for posteriors
Convergence settings (dlogz) significantly affect run time
best_when: Bayesian model comparison requiring evidence (log Z)
Multimodal posterior distributions where MCMC fails
When both evidence and posterior are needed simultaneously
avoid_when: Simple unimodal posteriors where emcee is 10x faster
When model comparison (evidence) is not needed
summary: dynesty provides dynamic nested sampling for Bayesian evidence and posteriors. DynamicNestedSampler(loglikelihood, prior_transform, ndim=5).run_nested(). results.logz for log evidence. results.samples for posterior. Multiprocessing. MIT by Josh Speagle. Use when Bayesian evidence (model comparison) or multimodal posteriors are needed. Slower than emcee for simple unimodal problems. prior_transform maps [0,1]^n to prior space.
